coredata.dc:title: Hydrogen sulfide gas sensing toward on-site monitoring of chilled meat spoilage based on ratio-type fluorescent probe
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Xiaowei
Sun, Wei
Li, Zhihua
Shi, Jiyong
Zhang, Ning
Zhang, Yang
Zhai, Xiaodong
Hu, Xuetao
Zou, Xiaobo
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a fluorescence sensing platform for visual detection of hydrogen sulfide (H2S) based on ratiometric fluorescent substances was developed to real-time monitor meat spoilage. The copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CNQDs) were used as dual emission fluorescence materials. The fluorescence ratio of the two wavelengths decreased in the sulphide concentration range of 0–3 μmolL(exp)−1, with a detection limit of 62.7 nmolL(exp)−1. In order to capture hydrogen sulfide gas in the air, the ratio fluorescent material is loaded on the paper base. By processing the RGB value of the photo under UV light, the detection limit of the sensor is 4.35 ppt in the range of 0 ∼ 45.2 ppt H2S gas concentration. This portable visual analysis greatly simplifies the steps of H2S gas detection while ensuring sensor stability and sensitivity. It also provides a new method for H2S detection during the meat storage process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratio fluorescence
Hydrogen sulfide gas
Visual analysis
Chilled meat
Spoilage
Paper-based sensors

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Article(s) from the Special Issue on Novel Food Analytical Methods by Dr. Edna Amanteand Dr. Hetong Lin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Visual detection of hydrogen sulfide gas by composite paper-based sensor
Analysis of volatile substances in samples under 4℃ storage environment
Determination of total viable count and H2S-producing bacteria count in meat samples during storage
Application of paper-based H2S sensor in monitoring meat spoilage
Analysis of selectivity and sensitivity of CuNCs/CNQDs probe for detecting sulfide
Construction of a ratio-based fluorescent paper-based sensor
Application of paper-based hydrogen sulfide sensor in meat spoilage monitoring

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Composite schematics of CuNCs, CNQDs, CuNCs/CNQDs nanocomposites, and paper-based sensors.

(a) the fluorescence spectrum of CNQDs and the dual emission of CuNCs/CNQDs; (b)TEM image of CuNCs/CNQDs nanocomposites; (c) Zeta potential of the CuNCs, CNQDs, CuNCs/CNQDs; (d) The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy(XPS) spectrum of the composites; (e) XPS spectrum of Cu 2p electrons; f, FTIR spectra of CuNCs/CNQDs, CuNCs, and CNQDs.

(a) Fluorescent spectra of CuNCs/CNQDs nanocomposites at concentrations of sodium sulfide from 0 to 3 μM; (b) CIE chromaticity diagram of the (x, y)color coordinates of the CuNCs/CNQDs nanocomposites varying with sulfide; (c) The linear relationship between the concentration of sulphides from 0 to 3 μm and the intensity of the fluorescence ratio (I645/I440); (d) The fluorescence intensity ratio(I645/I440) of different interfering substances reacting with CuNCs/CNQDs nanocomposites;

(a) Photographs of paper-based sensors exposed to different volatile organic compounds (VOCs) under 365 nm light for 48 h; (b) photograph of the gas sensor device; (c) At different concentrations of hydrogen sulfide gas, the (R-R0)/B value of the paper-based sensor varies over time; (d) In the range of 0 to 45.2 ppt concentration of hydrogen sulfide gas, the linear relationship between hydrogen sulfide gas concentration and (R-R0)/B value; (e) In the range of 0 to 45.2 ppt concentration of hydrogen sulfide gas, the linear relationship between hydrogen sulfide gas concentration and ΔE.
The color evolution of cuNCs/CNQDs paper-based sensors for monitoring the deterioration of fresh chicken breasts and pork at 4 ℃.

The (R-R0)/B value of the paper sensor with the changes of TVC for (a) chicken breast and (c) pork, and the (R-R0)/B value of the paper sensor with the changes of H2S-PBC for (b) chicken breast and (d) pork, during storage at 4 °C.
Comparison of previously reported methods and this work.

head.ce:title: Hydrogen sulfide gas sensing toward on-site monitoring of chilled meat spoilage based on ratio-type fluorescent probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Rd, 212013 Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Rd, 212013 Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Joint Research Laboratory of Intelligent Agriculture and Agri-products Processing (Jiangsu University), Jiangsu Education Department, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Joint Research Laboratory of Intelligent Agriculture and Agri-products Processing (Jiangsu University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International Joint Research Laboratory of Intelligent Agriculture and Agri-products Processing (Jiangsu University), Jiangsu Education Department, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Rd, 212013 Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a fluorescence sensing platform for visual detection of hydrogen sulfide (H2S) based on ratiometric fluorescent substances was developed to real-time monitor meat spoilage. The copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CNQDs) were used as dual emission fluorescence materials. The fluorescence ratio of the two wavelengths decreased in the sulphide concentration range of 0–3 μmolL(exp)−1, with a detection limit of 62.7 nmolL(exp)−1. In order to capture hydrogen sulfide gas in the air, the ratio fluorescent material is loaded on the paper base. By processing the RGB value of the photo under UV light, the detection limit of the sensor is 4.35 ppt in the range of 0 ∼ 45.2 ppt H2S gas concentration. This portable visual analysis greatly simplifies the steps of H2S gas detection while ensuring sensor stability and sensitivity. It also provides a new method for H2S detection during the meat storage process.

ce:section.ce:para: Meat and meat products are the main supply of basic nutrition for people because of rich protein and trace elements. However, the rapid growth of microorganisms consuming protein makes meat and meat products prone to spoilage. The advent of a cold chain could slow this down considerably. Chilled meat has become the mainstream of the meat market. Nevertheless, the cold chain does not inhibite all microbial reproduction such as Lactobacillus, Salmonella et al. remain microbial activity at 0 ∼ 4℃. They cause meat spoilage and produce hydrogen sulphide (H2S) when growing on chilled meat. hydrogen sulfide is a colorless, sweet, odorous egg-flavored irritating asphyxiating gas (Borch & Agerhem, 1992). In addition, Sulphurous compounds other than hydrogen sulphide have been implicated in a type of spoilage described as putrid, particularly associated with meat of a high pH-value (Mourão, Rebelo, Ribeiro, Peixe, Novais, & Antunes, 2020). So hydrogen sulfide can be used as an indicator of meat freshness especially of chilled meat (Zhai et al., 2019). For evaluation of meat freshness, traditional chemical methods, such as the determination of total volatile base nitrogen (TVB-N), played a crucial role in meat spoilage evaluation due to their relatively precise results, but they need to be conducted in specialized laboratories with time-consuming processes. Compare with TVB-N, H2S is a pure gas rather than a mixture and more suitable for no on-contact rapid nondestructive testing method. As meat spoilage is highly time-dependent, it is important to determine real-time meat spoilage (Lin, Yan, Lin, & Chen, 2014; Mourão et al., 2020). Therefore, it is of great significance to prepare a highly sensitive selective probe for on-line detecting gaseous phase hydrogen sulfide in chilled meat. The electronic nose technology (Li et al., 2019; Shu wang, Xiao wei, Zhen zhen, Tong tong, & Shuli, 2019; Sinju et al., 2022; Tian, Cao, Chen, Liu, Wang, & Gao, 2019; Zhang et al., 2019) are common rapid nondestructive testing techniques for gaseous samples. Metal-oxide–semiconductor-based electronic noses have high detection sensitivity, but cannot be used to detect H2S generated from chilled meat due to high operating temperature and large size. Visible light colorimetric technology is a particularly intuitive approach but it cannot avoid the error caused by environment light disturbance (Ahn, Han, & Lee, 2021; Kaushik, Ghosh, Singh, & Jose, 2018; Kim, Lee, Ahn, & Lee, 2019; J. Liu, Chen, Zhang, Gao, Zhang, & Hou, 2018; Paul, Sarkar, Sarkar, Barui, & Sarkar, 2020). The infrared spectroscopy is susceptible to ambient humidity (Tian et al., 2019). They are not suit for complex storage, transportation, processing and marketing environment of chilled meat. Among spectroscopy technology, fluorescent probe method is favored by researchers because of its high sensitivity and selectivity (Chen et al., 2021; El-Shamy, 2021; Ghosh & Biswas, 2021). Especially, ratio fluorescence probes are highly anti-jamming compared to single-emission signal-based fluorescent probes because they provide built-in self-calibration by calculating the strength ratio of the two fluorescent signals, providing more accurate quantification (Hu, Cao, Dong, & Tang, 2021). Although some organic probes have good detection characteristics, but its own biotoxicity has always been a food safety test must be considered (Li, Guo, & Lu, 2015). As a new type of fluorescent material, copper cluster has good prospects in the field of food safety testing because of its good emission signal, low biotoxicity, high selectivity and high sensitivity (Ling, Wu, Gao, Li, & Luo, 2015).Ma and his colleagues developed a low-toxic, simple, and inexpensive fluorescent probe using the unique optical properties of copper nanoclusters and good chemical stability (Ma, Chen, & Chang, 2014). Xue et al prepared a copper cluster, which showed that the ethanol solution could affect the interaction between the synthetic copper clusters. With the change of order between copper clusters, the fluorescence signal is different, and a simple ratio fluorescence sensing is obtained by using this phenomenon (Hu et al., 2021).However, the detection based on copper clusters and nitrogen-doped carbon points is mostly detected in the liquid phase and is not suitable for instant detection (Li, Yang, Wang, & Huang, 2021). This greatly limits the application of fluorescent probes, and portable fluorescent test paper can overcome these shortcomings due to its portability and simple preparation process (Wen et al., 2019).

In this study, a dual-emission fluorescent paper-based sensor was designed for ratio detection of H2S (Fig. 1 ). D(-)-Penicillamine (DPA) was used as a template to synthesize copper nanoclusters (CuNCs), which combine with carbon-doped nitrogen quantum dots（CNQDs)to form a CuNCs/CNQDs complex through electrostatic interaction. When the complex is exposed to hydrogen sulfide gas, the red fluorescent emission is extinguished due to the production of the Cu-S bond, while the blue fluorescent emission remains unchanged. Therefore, as the concentration of hydrogen sulfide gas increases, the fluorescence of the probe changes from red to blue. The probe was loaded the onto a paper base and prepared a portable fluorescent filter paper to detect hydrogen sulfide gas concentrations in meat spoilage.

d-penicillamine (DPA) purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Beijing, China), Copper(ii) nitrate hydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O), Sodium sulfide (Na2S·9H2O), Concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), PBS Buffer (1 × ) were purchased by Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure Milli-Q water (Millipore) was used throughout the experiment. All chemicals were at least of analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

Powder X-ray diffraction patterns (PXRD) were recorded with a Bruker D8 diffractometer system (Germany). The emission spectra of the synthesized materials were measured using the F–98 spectrofluorometer (Shanghai Leng Guang technology Co. Ltd, China). FT–IR spectrum was recorded with a Nicolet iS50 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100(HR), JEOL, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies (XPS) were obtained by Thermo ESCALAB 250XI (USA).

CNQDs are synthesized as follows: sodium citrate (0.1 g), ammonium chloride (0.53 g) and 5 ml water are mixed and transferred to Teflon stainless steel autoclave, which is placed in an oven at 180℃ for 4 h, and then placed at room temperature to cool naturally. The cooled mixture is purified with a dialysis film of 1000 Da to remove unresponsive precursors. The resulting product is centrifuged for 5 min at 7104 g to remove insoluble large particulate matter, and the purified CNQDs solution is kept in a 4℃ environment.

CuNCs/CNQDs are synthesized on the basis of previous studies (Ghosh & Biswas, 2021): under intense stirring, the copper nitrate solution (40 μl, 100 mmolL(exp)−1) was mixed with the 10 mmolL(exp)−1 penicillin solution with the purified 4 ml CNQDs, stirred for 10 min, resulted in white precipitation, red light under ultraviolet light, and stirred the mixture at 20℃ for 35 min. The resulting product is washed with water 3 times after centrifugation 10 min at 8000 rpm, then vacuum freeze-dried and stored at −20℃ for a long time.

Considering the instability of sulphides in the solution, a series of different concentrations of sodium sulfide solution are provided with PBS buffer solution. The 1 ml nanocomposite is mixed with a sulphide solution of 50 μl at different concentrations, and the fluorescence intensity of the different mixtures at 440 nm and 645 nm is recorded at the xenonex s 365 nm. And all relevant measurements are made three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of hydrogen sulfide gas by composite paper-based sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The CuNCs/CNQDs methanol solution (5 mg/ml) of 10 μl is dripped on the filter paper (d = 1 cm, d is the side length of the filter paper) and then dried on the paper base down with nitrogen. The paper base is placed in a custom-made acrylic containers for a transparent and sealed H2S gas generator. In order to generate gaseous H2S, different concentrations of sodium sulfide solution are added to the sample pool, and excess dilute sulfuric acid is injected into the sample pool by Micro syringe. Paper-based colors are monitored by Four-purpose UV analyzer and recorded on a smartphone. The concentration of hydrogen sulfide produced by the reaction process of sulfur ions and excess dilute sulfuric acid was calculated and the distribution of hydrogen sulfide gas in the gas phase and liquid phase was studied. Specific calculation process was shown in the Supporting Information online.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of volatile substances in samples under 4℃ storage environment

ce:section.ce:para: The 6 g sample was placed in a 15 ml headspace vial and then equilibrated at 60 °C for 15 min. A SPME fiber module (50/30 μm DVB/CAR/PDMS, 1 cm, gray; ANPEL Laboratory Technologies (Shanghai) Inc.) was used to extract volatiles from meat samples at 60 °C for 40 min.

The Trace Ultra ITQ1100 GC-MS system (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA) was used to analyze volatile compounds. The carrier gas is helium, and the carrier gas flow rate is 1.6 ml/min without splitting. The temperature of the injection port is 250 °C. Heating program: the initial temperature is 40 °C, keep it for 2 min, then increase to 100 °C at a rate of 5 °C/min, and finally rise to 230 °C at a rate of 6 °C/min, and keep it for 5 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of total viable count and H2S-producing bacteria count in meat samples during storage
ce:section.ce:para: According to previous research (Zhai et al., 2019), The study used the plate streak method to calculate the total viable count (TVC) and the number of H2S-producing bacteria (H2S-PBC) in meat samples. Meat samples (5 g) were mixed with 25 ml of PBS buffer solution, and the mixture was homogenized to extract the suspension. The appropriate concentration of the dilution (10 μl) was poured onto the surface of the medium, and the TVC was measured after culturing at 37 °C for 2 days. And the diluted solution was poured on the surface of Trisaccharide iron agar medium and incubated at 20 °C for 3 days to identify H2S-PBC. Bacterial counts are expressed in colony forming units (CFU) per gram of meat sample, and then converted to a logarithmic 10-based value, which is log (CFU/g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of paper-based H2S sensor in monitoring meat spoilage
ce:section.ce:para: Pork and Chicken breast were purchased from local supermarkets and kept below 4 °C. 25 g of fresh chicken breast and pork were put into disposable plastic packaging boxes. The paper-based sensor was placed on the bottom of the box and were stored at 4 °C.

DPA is used as a reducing agent for synthesizing copper clusters. The process is that SH is combined with copper, and the pH of the entire reaction environment is 2.66. Under this solvent condition, the COOH group is deprotonized to COO–, while the NH2 group is protonized to NH3 +. CuNCs and CNQDs have the opposite charge, so they are adsorbed together under physical conditions through electrostatic interaction. This nanocomposite exhibits two emission peaks at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. 2 (a)).

As can be seen from the (TEM) images, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots are bound together with aggregated copper clusters. (Fig. 2(b)) In the enlarged image, it can be clearly seen that the lattice distance of CuNCs/CNQDs is 0.535 and that of CNQDS is 0.224. Fig. 2(c) shows the potential changes before and after the mixing of CuNCs and CNQDs. In addition, Fig. S1 shows CNQDs and CuNCs/CNQDs in a dispersed state.

Fig. 2(d) shows an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of the CuNCs/CNQDs complex, which includes the combined energy of elements C, N, O, Cu, S, etc. Fig. 2(e) shows the energy level of the Cu element, with no Cu2p3/2 satellite peak near the 942 eV, thus proving that there is no existence of Cu(Ⅱ). There are 933.0 eV and 952.7 eV peaks in the spectrum, corresponding to Cu2p1/2 and Cu2p3/2 respectively (Jia, Yang, Li, Li, & Wang, 2014). Considering that the combination of 2p3/2 between Cu (0) and Cu (Ⅰ) can be relatively close, we believe that the valence of copper elements in CuNCs/CNQDs is between 0 and + 1 (Chen et al., 2021). From Fig. 2(f), we can further analyze the surface structure of CuNCs/CNQDs by the Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectrum. The sealing agent on the surface of CuNCs contains the carboxylate (COO–) and the amino (NH2) group, which is confirmed by the characteristic peaks of the COO–(1494 cm−1) stretch mode and the feature peaks of the NH2 (3410 cm−1) stretch mode (Jia et al., 2014). And the CuNCs/CNQDs surface contains the characteristic peaks of CNQDs, which include the feature peaks of the stretch vibration modes of CO (1610 cm−1) and CN (1406 cm−1) (Zhou, Jin, Zhao, Chu, & Peng, 2021). The feature peaks of these spectra clearly indicate the composition of CuNCs/CNQDs.

At 365 nm excitation wavelengths, CuNCs/CNQDs nanocomposites have twin emission peaks at 440 nm and 645 nm. During the assembly of CuNCs and CNQDs, the fluorescence intensity of CNQDs is reduced. This phenomenon can be interpreted as fluorescent resonance energy transfer (Li et al., 2020). And in the solvent environment with pH of 2.66, the electrostatic interaction between the two particles is maximized by the deprotonation and protonization of the cluster surface group. These effects greatly enhance the composition and arrangement of molecules and reduce the energy loss through the non-radiant rotational relaxation channel, thus significantly improving the emission efficiency (Jia et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of selectivity and sensitivity of CuNCs/CNQDs probe for detecting sulfide
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, we studied that the fluorescence changes of the combination of sulphides with CuNCs/CNQDs nanocomposites at different concentrations. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the CuNCs/CNQDs nanocomposite has two emission peaks at 440 nm and 645 nm. With the addition of different sulphides, the emission peaks at 440 nm remain almost constant, while the emission peaks at 645 nm decrease gradually, indicating that the fluorescence intensity of copper clusters is quenched by the influence of sulfur ions. As shown in Fig. S2, the peak corresponding to Cu 2p3/2 shifted to higher binding energy, accompanied by the appearance of a satellite peak at 942.08 eV, confirming that the Cu2+ was present after the reaction. Also, when the sulphide concentration reaches 1 mM, the fluorescence of CuNCs almost completely disappears and the fluorescent color of the solution gradually changes from red to blue, which is consistent with the CIE graph (Fig. 3(b)). In the range of 0 to 3 μmolL(exp)−1, I645/I440 is proportional to the concentration of sulphides, the correlation coefficient is 0.9923 (Fig. 3(c)), and the detection limit is calculated at 63.7 nmolL(exp)−1 based on a sign.

al-to-noise ratio of 3:1 (As shown in S7-S8). This shows that the detection sensitivity at low sulfur concentration is high, which has certain detection advantages compared with the previously reported methods (Table 1 ).

In order to evaluate the anti-jamming of CuNCs/CNQDs nanocomposites to other interfering substance, In the selectivity test in the presence of various interfering substances, the concentration of the interfering substances is consistent with the concentration of sulfur ions. As shown in Fig. 3(d), sulfur ions have a large change in the fluorescence ratio of the complex and the reaction to other interfering substance is not obvious. Therefore, we believe that CuNCs/CNQDs are highly selective in detecting sulfur ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of a ratio-based fluorescent paper-based sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
Inspired by the reaction results of CuNCs/CNQDs solution and sulfide ions, we further tried to construct a paper-based sensor to detect hydrogen sulfide gas. During the deterioration of chicken meat, various volatile gases other than H2S will be produced (Fig. S3). Among them, we selected some representative volatile organic compounds to test the selectivity of the sensor, including acetone, hexane, cyclohexane, chloroform, trichloroethylene, ethylene, isopropanol, methanol, ethanol, toluene, and trimethylamine. It can be seen from the figure (Fig. 4 (a)) that the probe has a certain selectivity to H2S.The CuNCs/CNQDs methanol solution (50 mg/ml) of 10 μl is dripped on the filter paper (d = 1 cm, d is the side length of the filter paper) and then dried with nitrogen. The paper base is placed in a custom-made ackli containers for a transparent and sealed H2S gas generator (Fig. 4(b)). In order to generate gaseous H2S, different concentrations of sodium sulfide solution are added to the sample pool, and excess dilute sulfuric acid is injected into the sample pool by Micro syringe. The concentration of hydrogen sulfide is controlled by the content of sodium sulfide (Table S1), and the paper color is monitored by a quad-UV meter and recorded on a smartphone. Therefore, we calculated the content of hydrogen sulfide gas in the atmosphere according to Henry's law (Eq. (1)).
(1)

The paper-based color gradually changes from red to blue over time (Fig. S4), and the captured image (R-R0)/B value stabilizes after 48 h (Fig. 4(c)). As shown in Fig. 4(d), in the range of hydrogen sulfide concentrations of 0 to 45.2 ppt, the paper-based image (R-R0)/B values collected showed a linear correlation with the gas concentration (y = 2.7032x + 0.0289, R2 = 0.9981), with a detection limit of 4.35 ppt (As shown in S9-S10). In addition, the reflected paper base is measured by a chromic aberration meter. We found that the paper-based color difference (ΔE) also has a linear relationship with hydrogen sulfide gas (y. = 13.5522x + 17.979, R2 = 0.988) (Fig. 4(e)).

Therefore, CuNCs/CNQDs paper-based sensors can not only improve the portability and practicality of The CuNCs/CNQDs probe, but also enable low-concentration hydrogen sulfide fluorescence quantitative detection and color-comparison semi-quantitative detection. Therefore, CuNCs/CNQDs paper-based sensors can not only improve the portability and practicality of The CuNCs/CNQDs probe, but also enable low-concentration hydrogen sulfide fluorescence quantitative detection and color-comparison semi-quantitative detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of paper-based hydrogen sulfide sensor in meat spoilage monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: Similarly, TVC and H2S-PBC in pork increased from 3.67 to 9.82 log (CFU/g), and from 3.47 to 9.74 log(CFU/g). A polynomial is used to determine the relationship between the number of microorganisms and the paper-based sensor (R-R0)/B value. For chicken breast meat samples, the quadratic equations between (R-R0)/B value and TVC (Fig. 6(a)) and (R-R0)/B value and H2S-PBC (Fig. 6(b)) were established, where R2 values were respectively 0.9757 and 0.9753. For pork samples, a similar quadratic fit was also established between (R-R0)/B value and TVC (Fig. 6(c)) and between (R-R0)/B value and H2S-PBC (Fig. 6(d)) Equation, R2 is 0.9447 and 0.9748, respectively. According to European Community legislation, 5 × 106 CFU/g, or ∼ 6.7 log(CFU/g), is the maximum acceptable amount of raw meat. Therefore, in this study, when the TVC of the chicken breast sample is 6.6 log(CFU/g), the (R-R0)/B value of the paper-based sensor is 0.0202 (Fig. 6(a)), indicating that if the (R-R0)/B value of the paper-based sensor is (R-R0)/B value is higher than 0.0202, the chicken breast cannot be eaten. When the TVC content in the pork sample is about 6.6 log (CFU/g), the (R-R0)/B value of the paper-based sensor is 0.0256. It shows that if the (R-R0)/B value of the paper-based sensor is higher than 0.0256, The pork cannot be eaten.

In summary, this study developed a simple fluorescence detection method for sulfur ions and a visual fluorescence detection method for hydrogen sulfide gas. First, we prepared a ratio fluorescent probe by electrostatic assembly, and the probe reacted with sulfide to generate CuS. The fluorescence of the CuNCs/CNQDs nanocomposites changed from red to blue as the sulfide concentration increased. The minimum limit of sulfur ion detection by this method is 62.7 nM. Secondly, in order to overcome the limitations of probe detection in solution, CuNCs/CNQDs were loaded on paper to achieve visual fluorescence quantitative detection of hydrogen sulfide gas and semi-quantitative detection of chromatic aberration. The ratio fluorescent paper-based sensor can detect hydrogen sulfide gas with a minimum limit of 4.35 ppt after being loaded by the probe. Then the CuNCs/CNQDs was applied to detect H2S generated from chilled chicken breast and pork during storage. It is showed good selectivity towards H2S against other volatile gases generated from chicken breast and pork during storage. The CuNCs/CNQDs presented visible color changes when it was used as H2S sensor to monitor chilled meat spoilage. This work opens up a way for the accurate and quantitative detection of hydrogen sulfide gas, and provides a reference for the on-line nondestructive monitoring of meat freshness.

Huang Xiaowei: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Sun Wei: Methodology. Li Zhihua: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Shi Jiyong: Validation. Zhang Ning: Validation, Investigation. Zhang Yang: Data curation. Zhai Xiaodong: Resources. Hu Xuetao: Data curation. Zou Xiaobo: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The National Natural Science Foundation of China (31801631, 1601360061), Jiangsu Natural Science Foundation for Excellent Young Scholars (BK20200103), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M683372), Jiangsu Association for Science and Technology youth Talent Promotion Project, The Earmarked Fund for China Agriculture Research System, Young talent Development Program of Jiangsu University. Science and Technology Innovation Agricultural Project of Shanghai Science and Technology Commission(19391902100).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.133654.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Ahn et al., 2021

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid and simple colorimetric detection of hydrogen sulfide using an etching-resistant effect on silver nanoprisms
Microchimica Acta

International Journal of Food Microbiology

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Borch, E., & Agerhem, H. (1992). Chemical, microbial and sensory changes during the anaerobic cold storage of beef inoculated with a homofermentative Lactobacillus sp. or a Leuconostoc sp. Int. J. Food Microbiol., 15(1), 99-108. 10.1016/0168-1605(92)90139-T.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Chen et al., 2015
